comment: I genuinely believed Google Glass just never came to market. Figured it was just a hypothetical tech demo that never launched.

I was so excited about these. Got to work with them on my job, and we were trying to make apps for them. Here's some notes of what I remember...
1) it's not made to run for very long. It gets REALLY hot REALLY fast. Hot enough to burn the skin if the screen stays on for too long.
2) If there was bright lights, it was practically useless.
3) It ONLY works on the right eye. If you have an eye condition where your right eye doesn't work (like me), you were shit out of luck. I actually talked to a Google rep in person and suggested a left-eye version and he literally rolled his eyes at me.

I'm not surprised it died.

The concept of a handfree device that feeds you information about your space is great for WORKPLACES, where expectation of privacy doesn't especially exist anyways. Checklists, duty rosters, inventory scanning, automated inventory transfer by just looking at a barcode for a few seconds or even taking a marker to the barcode and having an app recognize that as being 'removed from inventory'.

Like, productivity wise it's not a bad idea. Fuck social situations though, this isn't 'daily wear' this is 'work wear'.

I got to work with the 2021 Enterprise version of these last year as part of a research lab at my university. It did fold and had a better battery and processor. We created an app that helped blind people detect obstacles in front of them. Using it while testing over a few weeks, I found it to be very clumsy. The touch gestures worked inconsistently, the weight was annoying, and the screen was distracting. Overall it's a very interesting piece of tech and had a bunch of very successful research projects. One of them was using it to help doctors to get live notes of a patient visit. This project was well received, but never commercialized. I think all the good research applications were too niche to keep it a viable product.

The introduction of Google Glass at Google IO in 2012 was amazing. They jumped out of a blimp, rode mountain bikes on the roof, repelled down the side of a building, and rode through the auditorium to hand the glasses off as a live demo. Somehow that was the coolest thing these were ever able to accomplish.

With Apple making the same mistakes on the Vision Pro, it seems like Microsoft is the only one who figured out the secret: AR is not a viable consumer technology today, Enterprise applications are where the value is. Google learned that lesson far too late, and in a market they didn't understand at the time.

I worked for Augmedix for 3 years. Google Glass' most significant issues really mounted on three things. First, battery life was absolutely awful! Users had to carry around a bulky battery pack to keep it charged for an 8-10 hour shift. Second, heat would radiate from the temple area and cause a lot of people to become frustrated with the device, often wanting to remove it after wearing for 2 hours. Third, the wifi connectivity was awful! Always getting disconnected and annoyed in the entire process.

From the business side, it has an eye-catching appeal. Google Glass had such a splash on the tech market, and people really want to see what it looks like and what makes it unique.  I do think once they get their hands on it, they often feel let down.

comment:
The only reason why I’d probably get a pair of AR Glasses is for my job. Well, I’m young and don’t have a job, and I’m still in High School, but I have interests in Robots and 3D Mechanical Design and Engineering.

The thing is that I suck at 3D printing and most of the time my 3D prints fail either because a part is too big or too small in my mechanical machine.

With AR Glasses, I can actually see how big my parts are and how big they WILL be in real-life scale. That way I can plan ahead of time and make changes here and there.

I hope that this tech would get better and better, and that the tech would be cheaper than a mobile device soon.

I don’t care how ugly or beautiful the glasses are though. As long as they work!

comment:
Because of the aesthetic when worn, and the "always exposed" camera. Glass would never be successful in every-day/social life. You make yourself look kinda silly and stand out. On top of that , you put off your friends/etc. in social situations , as you are constantly pointing a camera at them. 
Its value will only be realized in situations where:
- both hands are needed 
- appearance does not matter to the wearer 
- situation is in a non-social environment
